2606.18057.title: When AI Says "I have been in similar situations": Synthetic Lived Experience in Peer-Like Caregiver Support
2606.18057.paper_summary: Caregivers often turn to online communities for informational and emotional support. In these spaces, peer supporters frequently draw on personal narratives to respond to emotionally complex caregiving situations. As LLMs are increasingly designed as peer-like sources of support, they introduce a critical tension: AI can provide immediate, private, and nonjudgmental support, but it cannot authentically possess the lived experiences that make human peer support meaningful. Yet, when prompted to sound peer-like, LLMs may generate language that implies lived experience. This creates a synthetic lived experience paradox: the same experiential language that may make AI support feel warm, relatable, and peer-like can also falsely position the system as someone with lived experience. We examine this paradox in the context of family caregivers of people living with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD). Drawing on caregiver support exchanges from online communities and prompted peer-like responses from three LLMs -- LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, and MedGemma -- we analyze how human peers use personal narratives and how AI incorporates similar narrative forms. Psycholinguistic analysis shows that peer responses used significantly more first-person and past-focused language than peer-like AI responses. Qualitatively, we identify seven types of personal narratives in human peer support and show that AI often captures their emotional work, but can fabricate experiential grounding. These findings reveal a narrative authenticity gap: peer-like AI can generate synthetic lived experience without the real experience that makes peer support meaningful. We argue that caregiver-support AI systems need mechanisms to distinguish supportive peer-like framing from fabricated lived experience, ensuring that models can offer warmth and validation without falsely positioning themselves as experiential peers.
2606.18057.paper_summary_zh: 照顧者經常轉向線上社群尋求資訊和情感支持。<lb/>在這些空間中，同行支持者經常利用個人敘事來回應情感複雜的照顧情境。<lb/>隨著大型語言模型（LLMs）越來越多地被設計為類似同行的支持來源，它們引入了一個關鍵的緊張關係：人工智慧可以提供即時、私密且不帶評判的支持，但它無法真實地擁有使人類同行支持有意義的生活經歷。<lb/>然而，當被要求表現得像同行時，LLMs可能會生成暗示生活經歷的語言。<lb/>這創造了一個合成生活經歷的悖論：同樣的經驗性語言可能使人工智慧的支持感覺溫暖、可親且類似同行，但也可能錯誤地將系統定位為擁有生活經歷的人。<lb/>我們在照顧阿茲海默症及相關癡呆症（ADRD）患者的家庭照顧者的背景下檢視這一悖論。<lb/>我們利用來自線上社群的照顧者支持交流和三個LLMs（LLaMA、GPT-4o-mini和MedGemma）所產生的類同行回應，分析人類同行如何使用個人敘事，以及人工智慧如何融入類似的敘事形式。<lb/>心理語言學分析顯示，同行回應使用了顯著更多的第一人稱和過去時的語言，與類同行的人工智慧回應相比。<lb/>在質性分析中，我們識別出人類同行支持中的七種類型的個人敘事，並顯示人工智慧通常捕捉到它們的情感工作，但可能虛構經驗基礎。<lb/>這些發現揭示了一個敘事真實性差距：類同行的人工智慧可以生成合成的生活經歷，而沒有使同行支持有意義的真實經驗。<lb/>我們認為，照顧者支持的人工智慧系統需要機制來區分支持性類同行框架和虛構的生活經歷，確保模型能夠提供溫暖和肯定，而不會錯誤地將自己定位為有經驗的同行。
2606.18057.author: Drishti Goel et.al.
2606.18057.authors: Drishti Goel, Violeta J. Rodriguez, Daniel S. Brown, Ravi Karkar, Dong Whi Yoo, Koustuv Saha
